back_translation: Therefore, what shall we say about what Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, has discovered?
For if Abraham was (royally) reckoned righteous by conduct, he would have something to boast about — but not before the (royal) presence of God
For what does the Scripture say? 'Abraham believed God, and (royally) it was reckoned to him as righteousness'
For the one who works, the wages are not credited as a gift of grace, but as a debt owed
But for the one who does not work — but believes in the One who (royally) reckons the ungodly as righteous — his faith is (royally) reckoned as righteousness
Just as David also speaks of the blessedness of the person whom God (royally) reckons as righteous, apart from conduct (according to the Law)
'The person whose transgressions have been forgiven, and whose sins are covered — he is blessed
The person to whom the Lord (royally) will not count sin at all — he is blessed'

Therefore, does this blessedness come only to those who are circumcised, or also to those who are not circumcised? For we have already said: 'The faith of Abraham was (royally) reckoned to him as righteousness'

Then in what state was it (royally) reckoned to him? In the time when he was already circumcised, or while he was not yet circumcised? Not when he was already circumcised, but while he was not yet circumcised

And he received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness by faith — the faith he had while he was not yet circumcised — so that he might be the father of all who believe even when they are not circumcised, in order that righteousness might also be (royally) reckoned to them
And the father of those who are circumcised — those who are not only circumcised, but also walk in the footsteps of the faith of Abraham our father — the faith he had while he was not yet circumcised
For the (royal) promise that was given to Abraham and his offspring — that he would be the heir of the world — did not come through the Law, but through the righteousness of faith
For if those who are under the Law are heirs, then faith is rendered useless, and the (royal) promise has been rendered void
Because the Law produces (royal) wrath — where there is no Law, there is no transgression

Therefore, the (royal) promise comes by faith, so that it may be according to grace, and may be firm for all the offspring of Abraham — not only to those who are under the Law, but also to those who are in the faith of Abraham. He is the father of us all

Just as it is written: 'I have set you to be a father of many nations.' He is our father in the (royal) presence of the God whom he believed — God who (royally) gives life to the dead, and (royally) calls things that do not exist to come into being like things that exist
Abraham believed — with hope, against hope — so that he would become the father of many nations, just as it had been (royally) said: 'So shall your offspring be'
And without weakening in faith, he considered his own body, which was as if dead, since he was about a hundred years old, and the lifeless womb of Sarah
But he did not waver in the (royal) promise of God by unbelief, but was strengthened in faith — giving (royal) glory to God
And being fully convinced that God (royally) is able to do what He has (royally) promised
Therefore, '(royally) it was reckoned to him as righteousness'
The words 'royally) it was reckoned to him' were not written for the sake of Abraham alone
But also for our sake — to whom righteousness will be (royally) reckoned — namely, we who believe in the One who (royally) raised Jesus our Lord from the dead
He was handed over because of our transgressions, and was (royally) raised in order that we might be (royally) reckoned righteous
